title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text:
Title:
Exploring Local Solution Manifolds of Nonlinear Least Squares Problems


Abstract:
This poster presents a characterization of the local solution manifold of rank-deficient nonlinear least squares problems, in which the Jacobian has constant rank in a neighborhood of a solution. We also present and an initial apprpach to computationally explore the local solution manifold. Parameter estimation problems in some computational neuron models lead to nonlinear least squares problems of this type. Identifying local solution manifolds provides important information to neuroscientists. The proposed characterization of the local solution manifold is based on the constant rank theorem and on a subset selection algorithm. A version of the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm using subset selection is used to compute local solutions. The approach is used to compute local solution manifolds in an HCN channel model.

title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text:
PhD student in Mathematics
Rice University
Email: fg50 [at] rice [dot] edu
CV: PDF
GitHub: fedor-gerasimov-50
LinkedIn: Fedor Gerasimov




I am a PhD student in Mathematics at Rice University, advised by Gregory Chambers.
My research interests are in discrete, convex, and computational geometry. I am especially interested in polyhedral geometry, unfoldings of convex polyhedra, and combinatorial/topological methods in geometry. See the Research page for more details.
Before joining the Mathematics Department, I worked in the CMOR Department at Rice University with Matthias Heinkenschloss on parameter identifiability and local solution manifolds of nonlinear least squares problems.
I received my B.Sc. in Applied Mathematics and Computer Science from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology in 2023. My undergraduate thesis, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii, studied blocking and covering numbers of convex bodies.




Exploring Local Solution Manifolds of Nonlinear Least Squares Problems

title: Research
text: My research lies in discrete, convex, and computational geometry, with a current focus on polyhedral geometry and unfoldings of convex polyhedra.

title: Research
text:
Current work
I am currently working with Gregory Chambers on problems in polyhedral geometry, especially unfoldings of convex polyhedra.
Some themes I am interested in include:

unfoldings of polyhedra
blocking and covering numbers of convex bodies
rigidity theory and applications of the theory of places

title: Research
text:
Previous research
Before joining the Mathematics Department, I worked in the CMOR Department at Rice University with Matthias Heinkenschloss on parameter identifiability and local solution manifolds in nonlinear least squares problems.
As an undergraduate at the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, I worked with Alexander Polyanskii on problems in discrete and convex geometry. My bachelor’s thesis studied blocking numbers of convex bodies.

title: Research
text:
Undergraduate and earlier projects

Blocking Numbers of Convex Bodies
Bachelor’s thesis, Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii.
Maximum-sum matchings of points
Undergraduate research project at MIPT, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii.
The project concerned maximum-sum matchings of points: given an even number of points in the plane, find a perfect matching maximizing the sum of lengths of edges connecting the matched pairs.
A conjecture of Andy Fingerhut from 1995 about ellipses induced by such matchings was later resolved by P. Barabanshchikova and A. Polyanskii in Intersecting ellipses induced by a max-sum matching.
Rigidity of Laman graphs and the Bellows conjecture
Thesis for the Research Practicum at MIPT, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii.
I studied applications of the theory of places used in the proof of the Bellows Conjecture. I was able to use this tool to find a proof of the rigidity of Laman graphs.

title: Teaching
text: I enjoy teaching mathematics through extensive problem solving and visual intuition. My teaching experience includes coaching students in preparation for national mathematics competitions in Russia, and serving as a teaching assistant for an undergraduate optimization course.

title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text: To Appear

title: Maximum-sum matchings of points
text:
This was an undergraduate research project at the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii.
Given an even set of points in the Euclidean plane, a maximum-sum matching is a perfect matching that maximizes the total Euclidean length of the matched pairs.
A motivating question, posed by Andy Fingerhut in 1995, asks whether certain ellipses induced by the edges of a maximum-sum matching must have a common intersection point. More precisely, for each matched pair, consider the ellipse with foci at the two endpoints and eccentricity \(\sqrt{3}/2\). Barabanshchikova and Polyanskii proved that the convex sets bounded by these ellipses have a common intersection point.
See Barabanshchikova and Polyanskii, Intersecting ellipses induced by a max-sum matching.
